## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set A]**

**Q1.** Define **Design of Experiments (DoE)**. List any **two advantages** of DoE over the One-Factor-at-A-Time (OFAT) approach in synthetic biology research.

**Model Answer:**

Design of Experiments (DoE) is a systematic, statistically rigorous methodology for planning experiments such that multiple input variables (factors) are varied simultaneously to study their individual and interactive effects on one or more output responses.

**Two advantages over OFAT:**

1. **Detection of Interaction Effects:** DoE uncovers synergistic or antagonistic interactions between factors (e.g., promoter × RBS coupling), which OFAT fundamentally cannot detect because it varies only one factor at a time.
2. **Experimental Efficiency:** A $2^4$ full factorial design needs only **16 runs** to estimate **4 main effects + 6 two-factor interactions**, whereas OFAT would require a prohibitive number of sequential experiments to gain the same information.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Set B]**

**Q2.** What is a **confounded effect** in the context of fractional factorial designs? Give one synthetic biology example.

**Model Answer:**

Confounding occurs in a fractional factorial design when two or more effects **cannot be estimated separately** from each other using the same set of experimental runs — they share the same numerical estimate and are said to be "aliased."

**Example:** In a $2^{4-1}$ design with generator $I = ABCD$, the main effect of A is aliased with the three-factor interaction BCD: $L_A = A + BCD$. For a synthetic biology optimization of four fermentation parameters (pH, temperature, agitation, aeration), if pH's effect is confounded with the three-way interaction of the others, the researcher cannot tell whether the observed effect is due to pH alone or to the combined interaction.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE**
> - Students **commonly confuse** main effects with interaction effects. Remember: the main effect of A is the **average** of all responses at high A *minus* the average at low A, regardless of B's level.
> - **Always state the hypothesis** (H₀: factor has no effect; H₁: factor has an effect) before computing F-ratios.
> - **Always interpret** the result in **biological context** (e.g., "Temperature has the largest main effect, suggesting that GFP folding kinetics dominate over transcription rate in this system") — purely numerical answers lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Units matter.** A response in AU (arbitrary units) is not the same as RFU (relative fluorescence units); mention units explicitly.
> - Forgetting to compute the **grand mean** before calculating effects results in cascading errors. KTU examiners specifically check this.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full 2² Factorial with ANOVA**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

A synthetic biology team is engineering a yeast strain for ethanol production. They investigate the effect of two factors using a $2^2$ factorial design with **two replicates ($r = 2$)**:
- **Factor A:** Glucose concentration (low = 20 g/L, high = 60 g/L)
- **Factor B:** Inoculum size (low = 2%, high = 8%)

The ethanol yield (in g/L) responses are:

| Run | A | B | Rep 1 | Rep 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | − | − | 4.2 | 4.5 |
| 2 | + | − | 5.8 | 5.6 |
| 3 | − | + | 6.1 | 6.4 |
| 4 | + | + | 7.9 | 8.1 |

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Calculate the main effects of A and B, and the AB interaction effect.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Perform a complete ANOVA and state your conclusions at the 5% significance level. (F$_{0.05, 1, 4}$ = 7.71)

---

**Model Solution for Sub-part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute run totals**

$$T_1 = 4.2 + 4.5 = 8.7, \quad T_2 = 5.8 + 5.6 = 11.4$$
$$T_3 = 6.1 + 6.4 = 12.5, \quad T_4 = 7.9 + 8.1 = 16.0$$

**Step 2: Main effect of A (glucose)**

$$\bar{Y}_{A+} = \frac{T_2 + T_4}{2r} = \frac{11.4 + 16.0}{4} = 6.85 \text{ g/L}$$

$$\bar{Y}_{A-} = \frac{T_1 + T_3}{2r} = \frac{8.7 + 12.5}{4} = 5.30 \text{ g/L}$$

$$E_A = 6.85 - 5.30 = 1.55 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 3: Main effect of B (inoculum)**

$$\bar{Y}_{B+} = \frac{T_3 + T_4}{2r} = \frac{12.5 + 16.0}{4} = 7.125 \text{ g/L}$$

$$\bar{Y}_{B-} = \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2r} = \frac{8.7 + 11.4}{4} = 5.025 \text{ g/L}$$

$$E_B = 7.125 - 5.025 = 2.10 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 4: Interaction AB**

Contrast:
$$C_{AB} = (T_4 - T_3) - (T_2 - T_1) = (16.0 - 12.5) - (11.4 - 8.7) = 3.5 - 2.7 = 0.8$$

$$E_{AB} = \frac{C_{AB}}{2r} = \frac{0.8}{4} = 0.20 \text{ g/L}$$

**Final answer (a):** $E_A = 1.55$ g/L, $E_B = 2.10$ g/L, $E_{AB} = 0.20$ g/L.

---

**Model Solution for Sub-part (b):**

**Step 1: Grand total and correction factor**

$$G = 8.7 + 11.4 + 12.5 + 16.0 = 48.6$$

$$CF = \frac{G^2}{N} = \frac{(48.6)^2}{8} = \frac{2361.96}{8} = 295.245$$

**Step 2: Total Sum of Squares**

$$\sum Y^2 = 4.2^2 + 4.5^2 + 5.8^2 + 5.6^2 + 6.1^2 + 6.4^2 + 7.9^2 + 8.1^2$$

$$= 17.64 + 20.25 + 33.64 + 31.36 + 37.21 + 40.96 + 62.41 + 65.61 = 309.08$$

$$SS_T = 309.08 - 295.245 = 13.835$$

**Step 3: Sum of Squares for factors**

$$SS_A = \frac{(A_+ - A_-)^2}{8} = \frac{(11.4 + 16.0 - 8.7 - 12.5)^2}{8} = \frac{(6.2)^2}{8} = \frac{38.44}{8} = 4.805$$

$$SS_B = \frac{(B_+ - B_-)^2}{8} = \frac{(12.5 + 16.0 - 8.7 - 11.4)^2}{8} = \frac{(8.4)^2}{8} = \frac{70.56}{8} = 8.820$$

$$SS_{AB} = \frac{(C_{AB})^2}{8} = \frac{(0.8)^2}{8} = \frac{0.64}{8} = 0.080$$

**Step 4: Sum of Squares for Error**

$$SS_E = 13.835 - 4.805 - 8.820 - 0.080 = 0.130$$

**Step 5: ANOVA Table**

| Source | $SS$ | $df$ | $MS$ | $F$ | $F_{0.05,1,4}$ | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A (Glucose) | 4.805 | 1 | 4.805 | 147.8 | 7.71 | **Significant** |
| B (Inoculum) | 8.820 | 1 | 8.820 | 271.4 | 7.71 | **Highly Significant** |
| AB | 0.080 | 1 | 0.080 | 2.46 | 7.71 | Not Significant |
| Error | 0.130 | 4 | 0.0325 | — | — | — |
| **Total** | **13.835** | **7** | — | — | — | — |

**Step 6: Biological Conclusion**

- **Factor B (inoculum size)** has the largest effect (F = 271.4), making it the dominant lever for ethanol yield optimization.
- **Factor A (glucose)** is also highly significant.
- **The interaction AB is negligible** (F = 2.46 < 7.71), confirming that glucose and inoculum size act **independently** in this yeast system. This is valuable engineering information: the two factors can be tuned **separately** in scale-up.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: RSM and Central Composite Design**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle of **Response Surface Methodology (RSM)**. Differentiate between a **Central Composite Design (CCD)** and a **Box-Behnken Design (BBD)** in terms of the number of experimental runs, the location of design points, and suitability for synthetic biology optimization of fermentation media.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following second-order model was fit to a Plackett-Burman-confirmed optimization of lipase production from *Rhizopus oryzae*:

$$\hat{Y} = 12.5 + 1.8X_1 - 0.9X_2 + 1.2X_3 - 0.7X_1^2 - 0.5X_2^2 - 0.4X_3^2 + 0.6X_1X_2$$

where $X_1, X_2, X_3$ are coded variables ranging from $-1$ to $+1$ (representing temperature, pH, and agitation rate). Identify the **optimum coded values** by setting the partial derivatives to zero, and comment on whether the optimum lies **inside the design space**.

---

**Model Answer for (a):**

**Principle of RSM:** Response Surface Methodology is a collection of mathematical and statistical techniques useful for developing, improving, and optimizing processes. RSM uses a **second-order polynomial** to approximate the true functional relationship between the response $Y$ and the independent variables $X_i$ over a region of interest, then locates the optimum using calculus or graphical techniques (contour plots, 3D surface plots).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Central Composite Design (CCD) | Box-Behnken Design (BBD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Design points** | Factorial + axial + center | Factorial (edge mid-points) + center |
| **Number of runs (for $k=3$)** | $2^3 + 2(3) + 6 = 20$ | $2(3)(2) + 3 = 15$ |
| **Axial runs** | Yes ($\alpha = 1.682$) | No |
| **Extreme combinations** | Included (all + / all −) | **Excluded** (safe for extreme conditions) |
| **Rotatability** | Yes (with $\alpha = 2^{k/4}$) | Yes (inherently rotatable) |
| **Synthetic biology use** | When extreme factor levels are safe to test (e.g., buffer pH range) | When extreme combinations could harm cells (e.g., high temperature + low pH together) |
| **Efficiency** | More runs but uniform precision | Fewer runs, edge-centred |

---

**Model Answer for (b):**

Set partial derivatives to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \hat{Y}}{\partial X_1} = 1.8 - 1.4X_1 + 0.6X_2 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{Y}}{\partial X_2} = -0.9 - 1.0X_2 + 0.6X_1 = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \hat{Y}}{\partial X_3} = 1.2 - 0.8X_3 = 0$$

**Solving for $X_3$ first (uncoupled):**

$$X_3 = \frac{1.2}{0.8} = 1.50$$

Since $X_3 = 1.5 > 1$, the optimum for $X_3$ **lies outside the design space** — this means the quadratic term continues decreasing beyond $X_3 = +1$, suggesting that the **true optimum is beyond the tested range** and the experimenter should expand the range of agitation rate.

**Solving the coupled equations for $X_1$ and $X_2$:**

From the first equation: $X_1 = (1.8 + 0.6X_2) / 1.4 = 1.2857 + 0.4286 X_2$

Substituting into the second:

$$-0.9 - 1.0 X_2 + 0.6 (1.2857 + 0.4286 X_2) = 0$$

$$-0.9 - X_2 + 0.7714 + 0.2571 X_2 = 0$$

$$-0.1286 - 0.7429 X_2 = 0$$

$$X_2 = -0.173$$

Then:

$$X_1 = 1.2857 + 0.4286 \times (-0.173) = 1.2117 \approx 1.21$$

Since $X_1 = 1.21 > 1$, the optimum for temperature also **lies outside the design space** on the high end.

**Biological interpretation:** The fitted model predicts that **lipase production would increase further if temperature and agitation rate are pushed beyond the originally tested ranges**. The student should design a **follow-up experiment** with a wider factor range, perhaps using a **steepest ascent path** to locate the new optimum.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision — bookmark this section.**

- [x] **DoE is a multi-factor, parallel, statistical method** — not a one-at-a-time sequential method.
- [x] A $2^k$ factorial design has **$2^k$ runs**; with $r$ replicates, total runs = $r \cdot 2^k$.
- [x] **Main effect formula:** $E_i = \bar{Y}_{i+} - \bar{Y}_{i-}$ (always difference of high-level and low-level means).
- [x] **Interaction effect formula:** $E_{ij} = \frac{C_{ij}}{2^{k-1} \cdot r}$ where contrast $C_{ij}$ uses the Yates algorithm pattern $(++), (+-), (-+), (--)$.
- [x] **Grand mean:** $\bar{Y} = G / N$ where $G$ is the sum of **all observations** across all runs and replicates.
- [x] **Correction Factor (CF):** $G^2 / N$ — subtract from $\sum Y^2$ to get $SS_T$.
- [x] **F-ratio = MS$_{factor}$ / MS$_{error}$**; compare to **F$_{0.05}$** at 95% confidence.
- [x] **Plackett-Burman** is for **screening** many factors (resolves only main effects).
- [x] **Fractional factorial** reduces runs by half (or more) but **confounds** higher-order effects.
- [x] **CCD** adds **axial points** at distance $\alpha = 2^{k/4}$ from the centre for quadratic modelling.
- [x] **Box-Behnken** avoids extreme corner combinations — **safer** for biological systems.
- [x] **RSM** uses a **second-order polynomial**; the optimum is found by **partial derivatives = 0**.
- [x] **$R^2$** measures model fit; **$R^2 > 0.85$** is generally acceptable in biological RSM.
- [x] **Confirmation runs** at predicted optimum are **mandatory** to verify the model.
- [x] **Resolution III designs** confound main effects with 2FIs — **avoid** for critical optimization.
- [x] **Resolution V designs** keep main effects and 2FIs unconfounded — **gold standard**.
- [x] **Taguchi methods** use orthogonal arrays (e.g., L4, L9, L16, L27) — popular in synthetic biology for robust design.
- [x] **Quality by Design (QbD)** framework uses DoE as a foundational tool — relevant for industrial biotech KTU outcomes.
- [x] **Randomization** of run order reduces the effect of uncontrolled nuisance variables (e.g., ambient temperature drift).
- [x] **Replication** gives the **degrees of freedom for error** — without it, you cannot compute F-ratios.
- [x] **Blocking** groups similar runs to control nuisance factors (e.g., different operators, different days).
- [x] For KTU exam numericals, **always show all 7–8 calculation steps** — partial work earns partial marks.
- [x] Always **interpret effects in biological context** — never present a bare number without biological meaning.
- [x] **Units, units, units** — state AU, g/L, RFU, mM, or °C explicitly in every numerical answer.

---

**End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Design of Experiment Approaches**
*PEBBT741 — Synthetic Biology | KTU 2024 Scheme*

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Synthetic Gene Circuits Encoded by Recombinant DNA** refers to the deliberate engineering of genetic regulatory networks using *in vitro* assembled DNA constructs that combine genetic elements (promoters, coding sequences, terminators, ribosome binding sites) from multiple biological sources into a single functional genetic unit. These circuits, once introduced into a host chassis (typically *Escherichia coli*, *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, or mammalian cells), execute user-defined logical, oscillatory, or feedback behaviours through the coordinated expression of recombinant genes.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEBBT741, Module 4), the topic encompasses:

1. The molecular biology of recombinant DNA assembly.
2. The abstraction hierarchy: **Parts → Devices → Systems**.
3. Canonical circuit motifs: toggle switches, oscillators, logic gates, and feedforward loops.
4. Quantitative modelling using Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** Students must be able to (i) describe restriction–ligation cloning and Gibson assembly, (ii) diagram a synthetic gene circuit using standard biological symbols (SBOL — Synthetic Biology Open Language), and (iii) write the governing ODEs for at least one canonical motif (toggle switch or oscillator).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Circuit Board"

Imagine a domestic electrical circuit board:

- **Resistors, capacitors, transistors** → in synthetic biology, these are replaced by **promoters, ribosome binding sites (RBS), coding sequences (CDS), terminators, and regulators** (repressors/activators).
- **Soldering wires** → in molecular biology, these are **phosphodiester bonds** formed by DNA ligase (or seamless enzymatic assembly like Gibson).
- **A printed circuit board (PCB)** → the assembled **plasmid vector** that carries the synthetic circuit.
- **The household appliance** (TV, washing machine) → the **host cell** that interprets the circuit and produces an output phenotype (fluorescence, growth arrest, metabolite production).

Just as an electrical engineer composes logic gates from transistors, a synthetic biologist composes **regulatory logic** from genes whose products (proteins) control the expression of other genes.

## 1.3 Definition Callouts

> [!NOTE]
> **Recombinant DNA (rDNA):** A DNA molecule formed by laboratory methods (restriction–ligation, Gibson, Golden Gate, SLIC) by combining genetic material from two or more sources that would not normally occur together.

> [!NOTE]
> **Synthetic Gene Circuit:** A rationally designed set of interacting genes and regulatory elements that implements a prescribed input–output behaviour inside a living cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Chassis:** The host organism (e.g., *E. coli* MG1655, *S. cerevisiae* BY4741, or HEK293 mammalian cells) that houses and executes the synthetic circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **SBOL (Synthetic Biology Open Language):** A standardized visual grammar for representing genetic parts, devices, and systems using glyphs for promoters, RBSs, CDSs, and terminators.

## 1.4 Why Recombinant DNA is the Foundation

Synthetic biology is *not* possible without recombinant DNA technology. Every gene circuit — whether a simple AND gate or a complex repressilator — relies on physically linking DNA fragments that originated from different organisms (e.g., a bacterial repressor gene *lacI* with a viral promoter and a fluorescent reporter *gfp*). Without rDNA, the precise spatial and stoichiometric control required for predictable circuit behaviour would be unattainable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Enabling Technologies:**
> 1. **DNA Sequencing & Synthesis** (reading and writing DNA cheaply).
> 2. **Recombinant DNA Assembly** (physically combining the written DNA into functional circuits).
> These two technologies, when combined with mathematical modelling, define the modern discipline of synthetic biology.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parts–Devices–Systems Abstraction

Synthetic biology adopts a hierarchical engineering abstraction borrowed from electrical engineering:

### Step 1 — Parts (BioBricks)
The **smallest functional DNA units** with a defined function:
- **Promoters** (e.g., $\mathrm{P_{lac}}$, $\mathrm{P_{tet}}$, $\mathrm{P_{trc}}$) — initiate transcription.
- **Ribosome Binding Sites (RBS)** — control translation initiation.
- **Coding Sequences (CDS)** — encode proteins (repressors, activators, reporters).
- **Terminators** (e.g., $\mathrm{T7}$, $\mathrm{rrnB~T1}$) — halt transcription.

### Step 2 — Devices
A **device** is a collection of parts that performs a human-defined function:
- A **NOR gate** device: an inverter whose output drives another inverter.
- A **PoPS-to-FL gate** device: converts a Polymerase Per Second input into a Fluorescence output.
- A **toggle switch** device: a bistable memory element.

### Step 3 — Systems
A **system** is an interconnected set of devices producing a complex phenotype:
- **Repressilator** — a three-gene ring oscillator producing sustained fluorescence oscillations.
- **Synthetic predator–prey ecosystem** — engineered microbial consortia.
- **Genetic counter** — a memory device that records environmental events.

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework — Why Models are Necessary

A synthetic gene circuit is **non-linear, stochastic, and embedded in a noisy cellular environment**. KTU examiners expect you to articulate *why* ODE modelling is required:

1. **Predictability** — design circuits *in silico* before expensive wet-lab work.
2. **Parameter sensitivity** — identify which knobs (promoter strength, RBS affinity, degradation tags) most influence behaviour.
3. **Robustness analysis** — ensure the circuit functions across variable conditions (temperature, growth phase).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Convention:** All variables below are written without the absolute-value pipe symbol `|` to preserve table syntax. Use `\vert` or `\mid` notation where ambiguity exists.

| **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Description of Variables** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hill Function (Activation)** | $\theta_a(X) = \dfrac{X^n}{K^n + X^n}$ | $X$ = activator concentration; $K$ = activation threshold; $n$ = Hill coefficient (cooperativity) | Models activated promoters (e.g., AraC–$\mathrm{P_{BAD}}$) |
| **Hill Function (Repression)** | $\theta_r(X) = \dfrac{K^n}{K^n + X^n}$ | $X$ = repressor concentration; $K$ = repression threshold; $n$ = Hill coefficient | Models repressed promoters (e.g., LacI–$\mathrm{P_{lac}}$, TetR–$\mathrm{P_{tet}}$) |
| **Transcriptional Rate** | $\mathrm{Production} = \alpha \cdot \theta(X)$ | $\alpha$ = maximal promoter strength (PoPS); $\theta$ = regulation function | Sets the synthesis rate of a protein |
| **Protein Mass Balance (ODE)** | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \theta(X) - \gamma \cdot P$ | $P$ = protein concentration; $\gamma$ = dilution + degradation rate constant | Governs dynamic protein levels |
| **Steady-State Protein Level** | $P_{ss} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \theta(X)}{\gamma}$ | Setting $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = 0$ gives the equilibrium | Predicts final circuit output |
| **Repressilator Coupling** | $\dfrac{dm_i}{dt} = \alpha_m \cdot \theta_r(P_{i-1}) - \gamma_m \cdot m_i + \delta_m \cdot m_i$ | $m_i$ = mRNA of gene $i$; $P_{i-1}$ = repressor protein of previous gene | Elowitz & Leibler (2000) ring oscillator |
| **Toggle Switch Steady State** | $\dfrac{u}{1 + v^{\beta_v}} = \mu_u \cdot u$, $\dfrac{v}{1 + u^{\beta_u}} = \mu_v \cdot v$ | $u,v$ = reciprocal repressor concentrations; $\beta$ = cooperativity; $\mu$ = effective growth/dilution | Gardner–Collins–Collins toggle |
| **Characteristic Oscillation Period** | $T \approx 2\pi \sqrt{\tau_m \cdot \tau_p}$ | $\tau_m$ = mRNA lifetime; $\tau_p$ = protein lifetime | Approximate repressilator period |
| **Plasmid Copy Number Constraint** | $\mathrm{Output} \propto \mathrm{Copy~Number} \times \alpha$ | Copy number = 1 (integrated), ~15 (pBR322), ~500 (pUC) | Tune expression strength via vector choice |
| **Fluorescence Normalization** | $\mathrm{Normalized~GFP} = \dfrac{\mathrm{GFP~signal}}{\mathrm{OD}_{600}}$ | Removes cell-density effects | Standard reporting metric |

## 2.4 Canonical Circuit Motifs — Operational Logic

### 2.4.1 The Toggle Switch (Gardner, Collins & Collins, 2000)
A bistable memory device composed of **two mutually repressing genes**, $lacI$ and $tetR$, each producing a repressor that binds the other's promoter.

**Logic flow:**
- State A: LacI HIGH, TetR LOW → cell "remembers" event 1.
- State B: TetR HIGH, LacI LOW → cell "remembers" event 2.
- Switching is induced by transient chemical inducers (IPTG or aTc).

### 2.4.2 The Repressilator (Elowitz & Leibler, 2000)
A three-gene ring oscillator: **TetR $\rightarrow$ LacI $\rightarrow$ cI $\rightarrow$ TetR**. Each protein represses the next gene in the cycle, producing sustained oscillations in fluorescence when a GFP reporter is coupled to one of the promoters.

**Why three genes?** Two-gene loops fail to oscillate because of insufficient phase delay; three or more odd-numbered genes are required.

### 2.4.3 The Genetic AND Gate
Output (e.g., GFP) is produced **only when both inputs** (e.g., arabinose AND aTc) are present. Typically implemented via split transcription factors or co-activator architecture.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biosensors:** Engineered promoter + reporter = living diagnostic (e.g., arsenic detector in *E. coli*).
- **Therapeutics:** Synthetic circuits in *E. coli* Nissle 1917 detect tumours and secrete anti-cancer payloads.
- **Industrial Bioproduction:** Dynamic regulation circuits decouple growth from production phases, increasing yields 3–5× over constitutive expression.
- **Cellular Computing:** Multi-input logic gates in mammalian cells classify disease states and trigger therapeutic responses.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Protein Level from Mass Balance

**Starting Equation (protein mass balance in a growing cell):**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \theta(X) - \gamma \cdot P
$$

**Step 1 — Identify each term physically.**
- $\alpha \cdot \theta(X)$ = production rate; $\alpha$ is the maximal synthesis rate from a fully active promoter, $\theta(X)$ is the regulation function between 0 and 1.
- $\gamma \cdot P$ = combined first-order loss rate, including proteolytic degradation plus dilution from cell division: $\gamma = \ln(2)/T_{doubling}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state assumption.**
At equilibrium, the protein concentration no longer changes, so:

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dt} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange.**

$$
0 = \alpha \cdot \theta(X) - \gamma \cdot P_{ss}
$$

$$
\gamma \cdot P_{ss} = \alpha \cdot \theta(X)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_{ss}$.**

$$
P_{ss} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \theta(X)}{\gamma}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The steady-state protein level is directly proportional to promoter strength $\alpha$ and inversely proportional to the effective degradation/dilution rate $\gamma$. This is the **engineering knob principle**: change $\alpha$ (swap promoter) or $\gamma$ (add a degradation tag such as ssrA) to tune output.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hill Function from Equilibrium Binding

**Goal:** Derive the probability that a promoter with $n$ cooperative repressor binding sites is *unoccupied* (and therefore active).

**Step 1 — Write the cooperative binding equilibrium.**
For $n$ identical repressor sites binding a repressor $R$ with microscopic dissociation constant $K$:

$$
K = \dfrac{[R]_{\text{free}} \cdot [S_{\text{free}}]}{[RS]}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the fractional occupancy.**
The probability that a single site is occupied by $R$ is:

$$
p_{\text{occupy}} = \dfrac{[R]}{K + [R]}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume strong cooperativity ($n > 1$).**
When all $n$ sites must be bound simultaneously to fully repress (an idealized assumption), the joint occupancy probability becomes:

$$
\theta_r(R) = \left(\dfrac{[R]}{K + [R]}\right)^{n} = \dfrac{[R]^n}{K^n + [R]^n}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute promoter activity.**
The probability that the promoter is *active* (no repressor bound) is the complementary fraction:

$$
\theta_a(R) = 1 - \theta_r(R) = \dfrac{K^n}{K^n + [R]^n}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The Hill function captures the **switch-like, sigmoidal response** characteristic of tightly regulated promoters. As $n \to \infty$, the response becomes a perfect step function (digital switch).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Toggle Switch Bistability Analysis

**Two coupled ODEs (Gardner toggle):**

$$
\dfrac{du}{dt} = \dfrac{\alpha_u}{1 + v^{\beta_v}} - \gamma \cdot u
$$

$$
\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{\alpha_v}{1 + u^{\beta_u}} - \gamma \cdot v
$$

Here $u = [\text{LacI}]$, $v = [\text{TetR}]$, $\beta_v$ and $\beta_u$ are cooperativities of repression, and $\alpha_u, \alpha_v$ are maximal expression rates.

**Step 1 — Impose steady state.**
Set both derivatives to zero.

$$
\gamma \cdot u = \dfrac{\alpha_u}{1 + v^{\beta_v}}
$$

$$
\gamma \cdot v = \dfrac{\alpha_v}{1 + u^{\beta_u}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve explicitly for $u$ and $v$.**

$$
u = \dfrac{\alpha_u}{\gamma \cdot (1 + v^{\beta_v})}
$$

$$
v = \dfrac{\alpha_v}{\gamma \cdot (1 + u^{\beta_u})}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute one equation into the other to eliminate $v$.**

$$
u = \dfrac{\alpha_u}{\gamma \cdot \left(1 + \left[\dfrac{\alpha_v}{\gamma \cdot (1 + u^{\beta_u})}\right]^{\beta_v}\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Graphical interpretation.**
The two nullclines $\dfrac{du}{dt} = 0$ and $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$ plotted on the $(u, v)$ plane produce **three intersections** when cooperativity is sufficiently strong: two **stable fixed points** (the two memory states) and one **unstable saddle point** in the middle.

> **Interpretation:** Bistability arises only when $\beta_u \cdot \beta_v > 1$ (sufficient cooperativity). Without cooperativity, the system collapses to monostability — there is only one memory state.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Repressilator Period Approximation

**Three coupled ODEs (mRNA + protein for each gene):**

$$
\dfrac{dm_i}{dt} = -m_i + \alpha \cdot \theta_r(p_{i-1}) + \text{noise}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dp_i}{dt} = -\beta \cdot p_i + m_i
$$

Here indices are cyclic ($i = 1, 2, 3$ with $i-1$ modulo 3), and time is normalized to mRNA lifetime $\tau_m$.

**Step 1 — Assume weak nonlinearity (limit-cycle small amplitude).**
Linearize each Hill function around the steady-state $p_0$.

$$
\theta_r(p) \approx \theta_0 - k \cdot (p - p_0)
$$

where $k$ is the slope at the operating point.

**Step 2 — Write the Jacobian matrix of the linearized system.**

$$
J = \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -\beta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -\beta \end{pmatrix} \cdot \text{(forward coupling)} + \text{(repression coupling term)}
$$

**Step 3 — Extract the imaginary part of the dominant eigenvalue.**
The characteristic equation gives oscillations with imaginary part:

$$
\omega \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\tau_m \tau_p}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert angular frequency to period.**

$$
T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = 2\pi \sqrt{\tau_m \cdot \tau_p}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Period scales with the geometric mean of mRNA and protein lifetimes. Doubling protein stability roughly increases the oscillation period by $\sqrt{2}$. This is why adding a degradation tag (e.g., LAA to ssrA) speeds up oscillations.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Recombinant DNA Assembly Workflow (Block Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical pipeline from isolated genomic DNA to a fully assembled synthetic gene circuit inside a host cell. This is the standard workflow tested in KTU Module 4 viva voce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[gDNA source A] --> C[Restriction digest or PCR amplify insert]
    B[gDNA source B] --> D[Restriction digest or PCR amplify vector backbone]
    C --> E{Assembly method}
    D --> E
    E -->|T4 DNA ligase| F[Ligation product: recombinant plasmid]
    E -->|Gibson 50C isothermal| F
    E -->|Golden Gate Type IIS| F
    F --> G[Transformation into E coli DH5alpha]
    G --> H[Selection on antibiotic plate]
    H --> I[Colony PCR and Sanger sequencing verification]
    I --> J[Validated recombinant plasmid]
    J --> K[Transform into expression chassis e.g. E coli MG1655]
    K --> L[Induce circuit and measure output e.g. flow cytometry]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Each node maps to a discrete wet-lab step. The yellow decision diamond (`E`) represents the choice of assembly method, which the KTU examiner frequently asks students to justify (e.g., *Why Gibson for fragments longer than 10 kb?*).

---

## 4.2 Synthetic Gene Circuit Architecture — Toggle Switch

This diagram renders the mutual repression architecture of the Gardner toggle. Read the arrows carefully: each repressor protein (LacI, TetR) inhibits the promoter of the opposite gene.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Promoter Plac] --> R1[LacI CDS]
    R1 --> T1[Terminator rrnB T1]
    R1 -. represses .-> P2
    P2[Promoter Ptet] --> R2[TetR CDS]
    R2 --> T2[Terminator rrnB T1]
    R2 -. represses .-> P1
    I1[Inducer IPTG] -. inactivates LacI .-> R1
    I2[Inducer aTc] -. inactivates TetR .-> R2
```

> **Reading Guide:** The dotted arrows represent protein–DNA repression (not transcription). IPTG binds LacI and relieves repression of $\mathrm{P_{tet}}$; aTc binds TetR and relieves repression of $\mathrm{P_{lac}}$. This is the **switching mechanism**.

---

## 4.3 Repressilator — Three-Gene Ring Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PA[Promoter PLtetO1] --> GA[TetR CDS]
    GA --> TA[Terminator]
    GA -. represses .-> PB
    PB[Promoter PlacI] --> GB[LacI CDS]
    GB --> TB[Terminator]
    GB -. represses .-> PC
    PC[Promoter lambda cI] --> GC[cI CDS]
    GC --> TC[Terminator]
    GC -. represses .-> PA
    PG[Reporter Promoter] --> GG[GFP CDS]
    PG -. coupled to .-> PA
```

> **Reading Guide:** The cycle `TetR → LacI → cI → TetR` is closed. The fluorescent reporter (GFP) reads the activity of one of the promoters, producing visible oscillations under time-lapse microscopy.

---

## 4.4 Parts-Devices-Systems Hierarchy (SBOL Abstraction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parts layer] --> B[Devices layer]
    B --> C[Systems layer]
    A --> A1[Promoter]
    A --> A2[RBS]
    A --> A3[CDS]
    A --> A4[Terminator]
    B --> B1[Inverter device]
    B --> B2[Reporter device]
    B --> B3[Toggle switch device]
    C --> C1[Repressilator system]
    C --> C2[Edge detector system]
    C --> C3[Synthetic ecosystem system]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This vertical hierarchy is the **engineering abstraction** that distinguishes synthetic biology from classical molecular cloning. KTU examiners expect students to identify *which layer* a given example belongs to.

---

## 4.5 Cloning Method Comparison Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Choose cloning strategy} -->|Short fragments and single insert| Q1[Restriction ligation]
```
